name: Nature of the Universe & General Scientific Laws

sourceRefs.label: civilserviceaspirants.in - Nature of Universe (Group 1 / Group 4-VAO question pages)
civilserviceaspirants.in - General Scientific Laws (Group 1 question pages)

researchNotes: Checked OCR index for 'universe', 'law of gravitation', "newton's law", 'kepler' — zero hits across all 730 question-bank pages, so this topic rests entirely on web research, flagged transparently. Raw pool was 18 distinct facts across two related syllabus clauses (Nature of Universe; General Scientific Laws) with no real overlap between them, so both were combined into one topic (each alone was a bit thin for a full reading/mock set) rather than padded separately. All 18 kept for reading; ~12 recast for mock, prioritizing the ones with the cleanest unambiguous answers.

reading.intro: Two adjoining one-line syllabus clauses — 'Nature of universe' and 'General scientific laws' — bundled into one topic since neither alone has enough distinct testable content for a full day. Universe questions tend to be single-fact recall (orbital periods, terminology); scientific-law questions tend to test everyday-life applications of Newton's laws, which is exactly how the syllabus itself frames this unit ('everyday application of basic principles of mechanics').

qa.question: How long does Earth take to orbit the Sun, and how long does Mars take?
qa.answer: Earth: 365 days. Mars: 687 days (about 1.88 Earth years, since Mars is farther out and moves slower).

qa.question: What is a galaxy, and what is the name of ours?
qa.answer: A galaxy is a system of billions of stars (along with gas, dust, and dark matter) bound together by gravity. Our galaxy is the Milky Way.

qa.question: What is the Big Bang?
qa.answer: The event when all matter was confined to a single point and began to expand outward — the leading scientific model for the origin of the universe.

qa.question: How did the universe's temperature change in the minutes and millennia after the Big Bang?
qa.answer: Within about 3 minutes, it had dropped below roughly 1 billion degrees. By about 300,000 years after the Big Bang, it had cooled further to around 3,000 degrees (the 'recombination' era, when the universe became transparent — note that cosmologists usually express this in Kelvin rather than Celsius, so treat the exact number cautiously, but '~3,000' is the figure these prep sources expect).

qa.question: What gases filled the early universe at that cooling stage?
qa.answer: Clouds of hydrogen and helium gas — the two simplest, lightest elements, which is why they were the first to form after the Big Bang.

qa.question: What is the only direct evidence of the Big Bang itself?
qa.answer: The Cosmic Microwave Background (CMB) — a faint glow of radiation filling all of space, left over from the early hot universe.

qa.question: What is an Astronomical Unit (AU)?
qa.answer: The average distance between the Earth and the Sun, denoted 'au'. 1 au = 1.496 × 10⁸ km (about 149.6 million km).

qa.question: The law of conservation of angular momentum is considered a consequence of which of Newton's laws?
qa.answer: Newton's Third Law — internal action-reaction force pairs within a system produce equal and opposite torques that cancel out, so the system's total angular momentum stays conserved when no external torque acts.

qa.question: In which everyday sport is the 'turning effect of force' (torque) directly used?
qa.answer: Cycling — pedalling applies a turning force (torque) to the pedal-crank, which is a textbook example of torque in daily life.

qa.question: Why do passengers lean forward when a moving vehicle brakes suddenly?
qa.answer: Inertia of motion — a body in motion tends to continue moving in the same direction/speed unless an external force acts on it; the passenger's body keeps moving forward even as the vehicle decelerates.

qa.question: Which theory explains the behaviour of matter and energy in black holes?
qa.answer: The Theory of Relativity (Einstein's General Relativity, specifically) — it describes how gravity warps spacetime so extremely that nothing, not even light, escapes a black hole.

qa.question: What physical quantity is defined as the product of a moving body's mass and velocity?
What everyday quantity directly changes a car's speed?
qa.answer: A net force causes acceleration, and it's that acceleration which changes the car's speed (Newton's Second Law, F=ma). Some prep materials phrase the question as simply 'what changes the car's speed' and mark 'acceleration' as the answer rather than 'force' — worth knowing both framings since the exact expected answer depends on how the question is worded.

questions.prompt: Earth completes one orbit around the Sun in approximately:
Mars takes approximately how many days to orbit the Sun?
What is the name of our home galaxy?
The event when matter confined to a single point began to expand, giving rise to the universe, is called:
The only direct observational evidence of the Big Bang is:
The average distance between Earth and the Sun is called:
1 Astronomical Unit is approximately equal to:
The law of conservation of angular momentum is a consequence of:
questions.explanation: Internal action-reaction torque pairs (Newton's Third Law) cancel, conserving total angular momentum.

questions.prompt: A passenger leans forward when a bus brakes suddenly. This is best explained by:
questions.explanation: Inertia of motion — the body continues moving forward as the vehicle decelerates.

questions.prompt: The Theory of Relativity is most directly used to explain the behaviour of matter and energy in:
questions.explanation: General Relativity describes extreme gravity, as found around black holes.

questions.prompt: Linear momentum of a moving body is defined as the product of its:
The early universe, shortly after the Big Bang, was filled mainly with clouds of which gases?
questions.explanation: Hydrogen and Helium — the two lightest, simplest elements formed first.
